questions.question: What is the evidence grade for intergenerational programs improving older adult wellbeing?

options.text: STRONG - multiple large randomized trials show consistent benefits
MODERATE-HIGH - systematic reviews show reliable positive effects
MODERATE-LOW - small and sometimes inconsistent effects in systematic reviews
WEAK - no credible research supports intergenerational programs

questions.explanation: The evidence grade for intergenerational programs is MODERATE-LOW. Systematic reviews suggest these activities may improve self-esteem, mood, and social outcomes for older adults, but pooled effects are generally small and sometimes not statistically significant. Benefits appear strongest when older adults feel recognized, useful, respected, and socially connected through meaningful contribution and mutual exchange—not just through presence alone.

questions.question: Why is acknowledging changed circumstances important in cross-generational wisdom-sharing?

options.text: Younger people need to learn that their circumstances aren't actually different from previous generations
Economic, social, and technological realities have changed—advice based on outdated contexts ignores systemic barriers younger cohorts face
Older people's wisdom is always outdated and shouldn't be shared across generations
Changed circumstances are irrelevant—human nature is the same across all generations

questions.explanation: Acknowledging changed circumstances is essential because economic, social, and technological realities have genuinely shifted. Advice like 'just work hard and buy a house' ignores changed housing markets and systemic barriers. Younger people resent advice that dismisses their different context; older people resent dismissal of hard-won wisdom. Both require acknowledgment. The solution is finding universal principles amid specific differences—not claiming either that everything changes or nothing changes.

questions.question: What is the difference between individualistic and collectivistic cultural framings of legacy?

options.text: Individualistic emphasizes personal achievement and unique contribution; collectivistic emphasizes family continuity and community service
There is no difference—all cultures view legacy the same way
Individualistic cultures don't value legacy; collectivistic cultures do
Individualistic is correct; collectivistic is a flawed cultural understanding

questions.explanation: Individualistic cultures (common in US/Western Europe) tend to emphasize personal achievement, leaving your unique mark, and individual contribution ('what will I be remembered for?'). Collectivistic cultures (common in East Asia, Africa, Latin America, Indigenous communities) tend to emphasize family continuity, community wellbeing, preserving traditions, and relational service ('how did I serve?'). Neither is more 'correct'—they're different cultural constructions of what matters in legacy work.

questions.question: What is cultural humility (as distinct from cultural competence)?

options.text: Mastering another culture through training so you can claim expertise
Ignoring cultural differences to treat everyone the same (colorblind approach)
Ongoing self-reflection about your own cultural location and staying a learner about others' perspectives
Only people from marginalized cultures can practice cultural humility

questions.explanation: Cultural humility means ongoing self-reflection about your own cultural location and openness to learning from others' cultural perspectives—without claiming to fully understand or master their experience. Unlike cultural competence (which can become a checklist of 'expertise'), cultural humility recognizes cultures are complex, dynamic, and internally diverse. You stay a learner, name your own cultural limitations, and invite adaptation rather than prescribing universal approaches.

questions.question: When does cross-cultural wisdom-sharing potentially cause harm and require stepping back?

options.text: Whenever you notice any cultural differences between yourself and learners
When you receive feedback that your approach is culturally insensitive, imposing values, ignoring power dynamics, or causing harm
When you use examples from your own cultural experience in teaching
Cross-cultural work always causes harm and should never be attempted

questions.explanation: Cross-cultural work becomes harmful when you're imposing your cultural values as universal, tokenizing or exoticizing people from different backgrounds, ignoring power dynamics (e.g., privileged position teaching as if experience is universal), or receiving feedback that your approach causes harm. Good intentions don't prevent harm—impact matters more than intent. When feedback indicates harm, listen, apologize, learn, adapt, or recognize some contexts aren't yours to teach in. Noticing differences or using your own experience aren't inherently harmful; the harm comes from treating your experience as universally applicable or ignoring feedback.

title: Intergenerational Connection and Cultural Bridge-Building - Quiz
